question: Why do parameterized queries ($1, $2) protect against SQL injection while template strings don't?

options.text: Parameters are passed separately from SQL -- the driver escapes them at the protocol level, not inserting them into the query string
Parameterized queries encrypt values before inserting into SQL
options.explanation: Values aren't encrypted -- they're passed separately via the PostgreSQL protocol. The DB receives the SQL template and values separately, so values are never interpreted as SQL code

options.text: Template strings (${ }) automatically escape quotes
options.explanation: Template strings DON'T escape anything -- they simply substitute the value. If userInput = "'; DROP TABLE users; --", it becomes part of the SQL code

options.text: Parameters only work with PostgreSQL, other DBs need a different approach
options.explanation: Parameterized queries are supported by all SQL databases (MySQL -- ?, SQLite -- ?, MSSQL -- @param). This is standard SQL injection protection

question: Why call client.release() in the finally block after a pg transaction?

options.text: To return the connection to the pool -- without release() the connection leaks and the pool gets exhausted
To close the PostgreSQL connection
options.explanation: release() doesn't close the connection -- it returns it to the pool for reuse. Without release(), the connection is considered busy, and new queries wait for a free connection

options.text: To automatically execute COMMIT
options.explanation: release() doesn't execute COMMIT -- you must explicitly call client.query('COMMIT'). release() only returns the connection to the pool. Without COMMIT, the transaction will be rolled back

options.text: finally isn't needed -- pg automatically frees connections
options.explanation: pg does NOT free connections automatically on errors. finally guarantees release() even on errors, preventing connection pool leaks

question: What formula is used to calculate the optimal connection pool size?

options.text: (cpu_cores * 2) + disk_spindles -- for SSD on a 4-core CPU, this is about 10 connections
Number of concurrent HTTP requests * 2
options.explanation: Tying to HTTP requests is a common mistake. The pool should be small: most queries wait on I/O, not CPU. The (cores*2)+spindles formula is optimal for PostgreSQL

options.text: 100 connections by default -- sufficient for any project
options.explanation: 100 is too many for most projects. Each connection consumes ~10 MB RAM in PostgreSQL. The (cores*2)+spindles formula usually gives 10-20 connections

options.text: 1 connection per application user
options.explanation: This would lead to thousands of connections, killing the DB. Connection pooling exists so a small number of connections serves many users through multiplexing

question: How do you prevent deadlocks in concurrent transactions?

options.text: Always lock resources in the same order -- sort IDs before SELECT FOR UPDATE
Use READ UNCOMMITTED isolation level
options.explanation: READ UNCOMMITTED doesn't prevent deadlocks -- it lowers isolation, creating other problems (dirty reads). Deadlocks are prevented by lock ordering

options.text: Increase lock_timeout in PostgreSQL
options.explanation: lock_timeout is the wait time for a lock, not deadlock prevention. It helps avoid hanging forever, but the deadlock still occurs. The solution is lock ordering

options.text: Disable transactions and use individual queries
options.explanation: Without transactions, data can become inconsistent (money debited but not credited). Deadlocks are prevented by resource sorting, not abandoning transactions

question: What are Prisma's advantages over raw SQL and Knex?

options.text: Full typing: TypeScript knows all field types and relations from schema.prisma
Automatic migration generation from declarative schema
Prisma always generates faster SQL than manual queries
options.explanation: Prisma can generate suboptimal SQL for complex cases. Raw SQL always gives full control. Prisma's advantage is DX and type safety, not performance

options.text: Include and select for loading relations and choosing fields
